Author: Homer
Greek_Text_Excerpt: αὐτίκ' ἄρ' εἰς οἶνον βάλε φάρμακον, ἔνθεν ἔπινον, νηπενθές τ' ἄχολόν τε
English_Translation: She cast into the wine a pharmakon: nepenthes and free from gall, a remedy for grief
Ritual_Context: None — domestic/social
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Grief-suppressing; death of identity/memory; forgetting
Notes: First documented drug transfer Egypt → Greece; nepenthes = 'no-grief'; ancient commentators id. as opium

Author: Homer
Greek_Text_Excerpt: δῶκε κυκεῶ τε... μίξασ' ἐν Πραμνείῳ οἴνῳ· ἀνέμισγε δὲ σίτῳ φάρμακα λύγρ'
English_Translation: Circe gave them a kykeon mixed in Pramnian wine and stirred in baneful pharmaka
Ritual_Context: Witchcraft/sorcery
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Men turned to swine = death of human identity; transformation rite
Notes: First appearance of kykeon in Greek literature; also in Eleusinian Mystery context later

Author: Homer
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ἀντίφαρμακον ὄπασσε... μῶλυ δέ μιν καλέουσι θεοί
English_Translation: He gave him a counter-pharmakon (antidote)... the gods call it moly
Ritual_Context: Divine gift (Hermes)
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: ANTIDOTE to Circe's death/transformation drug; primal antidote archetype
Notes: First use of ἀντίφαρμακον in Greek literature; establishes death-inducer/antidote dyad

Author: Homer
Greek_Text_Excerpt: τοῖον γὰρ φάρμακον εἶχε... Αἰγυπτίη δέ
English_Translation: Such a pharmakon she [Helen] had... an Egyptian woman gave it
Ritual_Context: None — hospitality setting
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Suppresses mourning/grief; makes men forget death of loved ones
Notes: Egypt as pharmacological source; parallels Moses/Egypt in Exodus

Author: Homeric Hymns
Greek_Text_Excerpt: κυκεῶ τεῦξαι ἐκέλευσε ἀλφίτῳ ἐν ὕδατι βληχῶνί τε μαλακῷ
English_Translation: She commanded them to mix a kykeon: barley in water with soft pennyroyal
Substance_Described: Kykeon (κυκεών): barley + water + pennyroyal + possibly ergot
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Hordeum vulgare; Mentha pulegium; Claviceps purpurea (hypothetical)
Ritual_Context: Eleusinian Mystery initiatory drink
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Demeter REFUSES wine (Dionysus's substance); ACCEPTS kykeon; initiation into death/rebirth mystery
Notes: Wasson-Hofmann-Ruck hypothesis (1978): ergotized barley produced LSD-like alkaloids; founding document of Eleusinian pharmacology

Author: Pindar
Greek_Text_Excerpt: πολυφάρμακοι Μήδειαν... θελξίμβροτοι ἐπᾳοιδαί
English_Translation: Medea skilled in many drugs... with man-enchanting chants
Ritual_Context: Colchian magical tradition; Hecate worship
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Medea = ur-pharmacist; drugs inseparable from chants/incantations
Notes: Polypharmakos as honorific; same term used for Circe (Od.10.276); both female figures at geographical margins

Author: Pindar
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ὁ δὲ καὶ τὸν ἐκεῖθεν ἀμαχανίᾳ δεδεμένον ἤδη τιν' ἄνδρ' ἐκ θανάτου κόμισεν
English_Translation: He [Asclepius] brought back from death a man already bound in hopeless bonds
Ritual_Context: Medical-divine practice; Asclepius as son of Apollo
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Direct RESURRECTION by pharmacological means; Zeus destroys Asclepius for it = death for conquering death
Notes: Structural parallel to Jesus: healer who raises dead is himself killed; pharmacological resurrection as transgression

Author: Euripides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ἐκ τῆς γὰρ Ἑκάτης Φεραίας, ἣν ἐγὼ πάντων μάλιστα δαιμόνων σέβω ξύνεργον
English_Translation: From Hecate of Pherae whom I revere above all divine powers as my collaborator
Ritual_Context: Hecate worship; chthonic religion
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Medea's power over life/death derived from Hecate — goddess of underworld pharmaka
Notes: Hecate = divine source of both death-drugs and antidotes; triple-goddess of crossroads/death

Author: Euripides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose summary] The golden robe and crown adhere and burn; Glauce and Creon die in agony
English_Translation: Poisoned robe/crown applied via anointing
Substance_Described: Contact poison (aconite in oil? phosphorus compound?)
Ritual_Context: Anointing garment = death by χρίω; direct inversion of royal anointing; anointed = killed
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Pharmacological inversion of χριστός: the robe that anoints unto death vs. the anointing that consecrates unto life

Author: Euripides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ὁ δ' αὖ θεὸς Βρόμιος οἶνον ἀνθρώποις φέρει· τούτου θανόντος οὐκέτ' ἔστιν οὐδ' ἐρᾶν
English_Translation: The god Bromios brings wine to men; if this [wine] dies there is no more love or joy
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Vitis vinifera (ethanol; also possibly ivy/other additives)
Ritual_Context: Dionysiac thiasos; initiatory frenzy
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Wine as life-giver and death-inducer; sparagmos (tearing apart) = symbolic death; ecstatic return = rebirth
Notes: Bacchae dramatizes full death/resurrection pattern through wine-pharmakon; Agave kills son in Dionysiac frenzy

Author: Euripides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose summary] Agave tears off Pentheus's head believing him a lion — sparagmos
English_Translation: Madness induced by Dionysiac possession (θεία μανία)
Ritual_Context: Tearing apart (σπαραγμός) = ritual death; eating raw flesh (ὠμοφαγία) = communion with dead god
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Dionysus himself was torn apart by Titans and resurrected; Bacchic rite re-enacts this

Author: Sophocles
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Nessus tells Deianira his blood is a love-potion (φίλτρον); it is Hydra-venom contact poison
English_Translation: Hydra venom / Nessus's blood (presented as φίλτρον / love charm)
Ritual_Context: Poison presented as love-potion: death-inducer disguised as fertility/love charm; no antidote = tragedy
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Perfect structural inversion: pharmakological deception where the healer (love) is actually death; Heracles cannot die gloriously, burns alive

Author: Herodotus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [Greek text: σπέρματα τῆς κανναβίδος ἐπιβάλλουσι τοῖσι διαπύροισι λίθοισι... ὑπεκδύντες δὲ τῷ εἰρίνῳ φεύγουσι]
English_Translation: They throw hemp seeds onto red-hot stones under felt tents; the vapor exceeds any Greek bath; they howl with delight
Ritual_Context: Scythian funerary purification rite
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Psychoactive vapor used in rites surrounding death; ecstatic response (ὀλολύζουσι) = ritual cry
Notes: EARLIEST documented psychoactive ritual use in Western literature; funerary context = substance mediates between living and dead

Author: Herodotus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose: Enarees divine using willow rods and linden bark; Aphrodite gave them the art]
English_Translation: Hemp (implied; combined with 4.73-75)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Scythian divination; Enarees as androgynous shamanic diviners
Ritual_Context: Divination = accessing death-world knowledge; hemp enables contact with the divine
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Scythian hemp cult has divinatory; purificatory; and funerary dimensions = complete religious pharmacological system

Author: Herodotus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Cyrus prepared a feast with unwatered wine (ἄκρητος οἶνος) as military trap for Massagetae
English_Translation: Wine (ἄκρητος οἶνος — unmixed/unwatered wine)
Ritual_Context: Wine as incapacitating weapon = pharmacological warfare; Tomyris warned not to use it
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Greek culture traditionally diluted wine with water; unwatered wine = barbaric excess and pharmacological danger

Author: Plato
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Socrates drinks the pharmakon; numbness ascends from feet to heart; he dies saying 'I owe a cock to Asclepius'
English_Translation: Hemlock (κώνειον)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Philosophical: death as initiation (μελέτη θανάτου — practice for dying)
Ritual_Context: DEATH by pharmakon framed as philosophical/mystical initiation; debt to Asclepius = healing-god; death as cure
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Clinically accurate description of conium alkaloid poisoning; ascending paralysis; Socrates = philosopher-initiate; dying as the final mystery

Author: Plato
Greek_Text_Excerpt: τὸ φύλλον δύναμίν τινα εἶχεν, ἀλλὰ χωρὶς τῆς ἐπᾠδῆς οὐδὲν ὄφελος εἶναι
English_Translation: The leaf had a certain power, but without the charm it was of no use
Ritual_Context: Medical-magical unity: drug + incantation = complete pharmacological act
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Drug requires ritual accompaniment; pharmakological and magical are unified
Notes: Confirms that Greek pharmaka regularly required ritual framing — drug alone insufficient

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: τρεῖς κύκλους ξίφει περιγράψαντα δεῖν ἐκτεμεῖν πρὸς ἑσπέραν ὁρῶντα
English_Translation: One must cut [mandrake] facing west after drawing three circles with a sword
Ritual_Context: Rhizotomoi harvest ritual: three circles; west-facing (land of dead); erotic speech; circumambulation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Ritual harvesting = pharmacological act is simultaneously religious act; west = realm of dead = death-orientation
Notes: First detailed description of ritual pharmacological harvesting in Greek botanical literature

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: δίδοσθαι δὲ καὶ ἐπὶ τοῦ ἀνωδύνου τοῖς τεμνομένοις ἢ καιομένοις
English_Translation: [Mandrake] is given as an anesthetic to those being cut or cauterized
Ritual_Context: Medical (surgical anesthesia)
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: First description of surgical anesthesia in Western medical literature; wine as delivery vehicle
Notes: Mandrake produces death-like sleep from which patient awakens — direct death/resurrection structure in medical procedure

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: λέγουσι δ' ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ Κερβέρου στόματος ἐρρύη τὸ ἀκόνιτον
English_Translation: They say aconite flowed from the mouth of Cerberus [when Heracles dragged him from Hades]
Ritual_Context: Chthonic mythological origin; Hecate's substance
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Drug originates in the underworld itself (Cerberus = guardian of death); aconite = death in plant form
Notes: Origin myth locates substance at boundary of life and death; harvested from underworld; Hecate connection

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: τοῖς δὲ χρωμένοις ἐπὶ θανάτῳ σήσαμον καὶ οἶνον προστιθέασιν ὅπως βραδύτερον ἀποθνῄσκωσιν
English_Translation: Those who use [aconite] for death add sesame and wine so the person dies more slowly
Ritual_Context: Execution / poisoning
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Explicit dose-timing pharmacology for death management: retardants used to slow aconite death
Notes: Earliest documented pharmacological death-timing manipulation in Greek literature

Author: Apollonius Rhodius
Greek_Text_Excerpt: νυκτὶ δ' ἐν ὀρφναίῃ φάρμακον ἔντυεν... τὸ καλέουσι Προμήθειον... ἄνθος ἐρυθρόν
English_Translation: In the dark of night she prepared the drug they call Promethean... its flower blood-red
Substance_Described: Promethean drug (from Prometheus's blood; harvested at midnight)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Unknown; possibly aconite (blood-red flower); or Paeonia officinalis
Ritual_Context: Ritual: midnight harvest (Hecate's hour); applied as body ointment (χριστικόν)
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Drug ANOINTED onto Jason's body as protective χρίσμα — anointing substance that prevents death
Notes: DIRECT CONNECTION to χρίω: Medea anoints Jason; the drug-anointing makes him invulnerable; same root as χριστός

Author: Apollonius Rhodius
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ἐπ' Αἰήτεω θυγατρί, Ἑκάτης μεγάλης δεδαημένῃ φάρμακα κείνης
English_Translation: From Hecate the great, from whom she [Medea] had learned her pharmaka
Ritual_Context: Hecate worship / initiation into chthonic pharmacology
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Hecate as original teacher of pharmaka; divine-to-human transmission of death-knowledge
Notes: Medea as Hecate's human initiate; pharmacological knowledge as mystery-cult initiation

Author: Apollonius Rhodius
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] Medea prepares sleep-inducing drug for the dragon guarding the Golden Fleece
English_Translation: Sleep-inducer for dragon (juniper? opium? mandrake?)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Ritual of access to sacred object
Ritual_Context: Dragon put to SLEEP (death-like state) to allow theft of divine object; sleep = temporary death
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Sleep of dragon = temporary death; Jason's passage = initiatory journey through death

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] ὄπιον... ὑπνωτικόν ἐστι· πλεῖον δὲ λαμβανόμενον θανάσιμόν ἐστι
English_Translation: Opium is soporific; if taken in excess it is lethal
Ritual_Context: Medical; but key component of Mithridatic antidote (theriac)
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Death by opium = sleep; small dose = healing sleep; large dose = permanent sleep (death)
Notes: Dioscorides contemporary with early Christianity; documents the substance central to theriac/antidote tradition

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] ναρκωτικὴν καὶ καρωτικὴν δύναμιν ἔχει... μανίαν ποιεῖ... θανατηφόρον
English_Translation: [Henbane] has narcotic and benumbing power... causes madness... [excess is] death-bringing
Ritual_Context: Potential Dionysiac? hallucinogenic-narcotic
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Madness → death spectrum: small dose = narcosis; medium dose = hallucination (Dionysiac frenzy?); large dose = death
Notes: Contains scopolamine and hyoscine; anticholinergic hallucinations; possible use in mystery cult contexts

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] οἶνος μανδραγορίτης... τοῖς τεμνομένοις ἢ καιομένοις, ὅπως ἀναίσθητοι γένωνται
English_Translation: Wine of mandrake... given to those being cut or cauterized so they may be without sensation
Ritual_Context: Medical anesthesia; possible ritual use
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Wine of mandrake = death-like sleep → surgical procedure → awakening = resurrection structure
Notes: Delivery via wine sponge theoretically possible; directly relevant to sponge episode in Passion narrative

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] ἀκόνιτον... λέγεται δὲ αὐτὸ ἀπὸ τοῦ Κερβέρου τοῦ ᾅδου σιέλου φύναι κατ' Ἑκάτης
English_Translation: Aconite... said to grow from the spittle of Cerberus of Hades according to [the myth of] Hecate
Ritual_Context: Chthonic origin myth; Hecate; used as assassination poison
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Myth of underworld origin repeated in medical text = drug of death/Hecate fully canonical by 1st c. CE
Notes: By Dioscorides's time the mythological-pharmacological framework is stable and widely known

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] κώνειον... ψυχρὸν τὴν δύναμιν... ἐπὶ κόλασιν κατεχρήσαντο Ἀθηναῖοι
English_Translation: Hemlock... cold in power... the Athenians used it for punishment
Ritual_Context: Athenian execution rite; Socratic initiation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Institutionalized death by pharmakon; cold = absence of life-heat; ascending paralysis
Notes: First century CE medical text confirms hemlock's institutional death-role; links to Phaedo and philosophical death-as-initiation

Author: Dioscorides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] ἐλλέβορος λευκός... ἄνω καθαίρει... ἐν μεγίστῃ δόσει θανατηφόρος
English_Translation: White hellebore... purges upward [emetic]... in greatest dose death-bringing
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Helleborus niger / H. orientalis; Veratrum album
Ritual_Context: Cure for madness (Melampus tradition); purgative-cathartic initiation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Purge as death-like crisis → restoration of sanity = resurrection pattern; large dose = death
Notes: Melampus cured mad daughters of Proetus with hellebore = founding myth of Greek psychiatry

Author: Pliny the Elder
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [Latin] Mithridates... venena cotidie potavit praesumens remedia, ut consuetudine ipsa innoxia fierent
English_Translation: Mithridates drank poisons daily, taking antidotes in advance, so that by habit they became harmless
Ritual_Context: Royal self-immunization program; implicit myth of death-immunity
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Man who cannot die: Mithridates made himself pharmacologically deathproof; structural resurrection mythology
Notes: When defeated, Mithridates could not poison himself — had to be run through by a soldier; ironic inversion

Author: Pliny the Elder
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [Latin] theriacam Andromachi... ab Mithridate rege institutam
English_Translation: The theriac of Andromachus... instituted by King Mithridates
Ritual_Context: Medical prophylaxis; royal-medical tradition
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Antidote as protection against ALL death by poison = pharmacological immortality claim
Notes: Pliny documents Mithridatic antidote tradition fully; opium as central = the death-drug is also the antidote

Author: Galen
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] ἡ θηριακὴ ἀντίδοτος... Μιθριδάτης ὁ βασιλεὺς πρῶτος συνέταξεν
English_Translation: The theriac antidote... King Mithridates first composed it
Ritual_Context: Imperial medicine: Galen prepares for Marcus Aurelius personally
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Universal antidote = pharmacological defense against death; opium in antidote = death-drug becomes life-preserver
Notes: Galen traces explicit lineage: Mithridates → Andromachus (Nero's physician) → Galen

Author: Galen
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] τὴν ψυχὴν ἀπολλυμένην... ἡ θηριακὴ σώζει
English_Translation: The theriac saves the soul/life from perishing
Ritual_Context: Medical; implicit mythological framing of death-prevention
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Galen explicitly frames theriac as salvation from death — religious language for pharmacological protection
Notes: 'Saves the soul' = σώζει; same verb as Christian soteriology; pharmacological salvation vs. spiritual salvation

Author: Galen
Work_Title: Περὶ κράσεως καὶ δυνάμεως τῶν ἁπλῶν φαρμάκων
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose: systematic description of drug properties by temperament — hot/cold/wet/dry]
English_Translation: Mandrake; opium; henbane; aconite; hellebore
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Medical classification; no explicit ritual context
Ritual_Context: Each drug analyzed for its capacity to produce death-like states (cooling = reduced vitality) or restoration
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Galen's temperature metaphysics: cold = death-like; warm = life-affirming; drugs modulate on life/death axis

Author: Plutarch
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] τὸν Ὄσιριν ἀναστῆσαι φαρμάκοις... εἰς ταὐτὸ συνάγει τοῖς Διονύσου μύθοις
English_Translation: Isis raised Osiris with pharmaka... [Plutarch] aligns this with the myths of Dionysus
Ritual_Context: Cross-cultural mystery religion interpretation: Osiris = Dionysus = dying-rising god
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Isis uses PHARMAKA in resurrection of Osiris; direct pharmacological resurrection event; Dionysus parallel explicit
Notes: Plutarch's comparative religion confirms dying/rising god + pharmaka = universal mystery religion structure

Author: Clement of Alexandria
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ἐνήστευσα, ἔπιον τὸν κυκεῶνα, ἔλαβον ἐκ κίστης· ἐργασάμενος ἀπεθέμην εἰς κάλαθον
English_Translation: I fasted; I drank the kykeon; I took from the chest; having performed the task I placed in the basket
Ritual_Context: Eleusinian Mystery initiation ritual (synthema = password)
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Initiate DRINKS kykeon as central ritual act; this mediates death/rebirth experience
Notes: Clement reveals Eleusinian synthema as Christian polemic; confirms kykeon as the initiatory substance

Author: Pausanias
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ὄναρ δέ μοι κεκώλυκεν ἐς τὸν ἐντὸς τείχους γράφειν χῶρον
English_Translation: A dream forbade me to describe the things within the sanctuary wall
Ritual_Context: Eleusinian sanctuary: operative secrets; initiation as death/rebirth
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Pausanias himself an initiate; prohibition on revelation confirms pharmacological secrets were guarded
Notes: Sanctuary secrets + initiatory experience = what cannot be said; the kykeon experience was too transformative to describe

Author: Orphic Gold Tablets (Pelinna)
Greek_Text_Excerpt: νῦν ἔθανες καὶ νῦν ἐγένου, τρισόλβιε, ἄματι τῷδε. εἰπεῖν Φερσεφόναι σ' ὅτι Βάκχιος αὐτὸς ἔλυσε
English_Translation: Now you have died and now you have been born, O thrice happy, on this same day. Tell Persephone that Bacchios himself released you
Substance_Described: Implicit pharmacological initiation (no specific drug named)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Possibly wine; ergot; other Dionysiac substances
Ritual_Context: Orphic/Bacchic funerary initiation; underworld navigation instructions
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: DEATH AND REBIRTH ON SAME DAY; Dionysus as liberator from death; direct resurrection formula
Notes: Structurally parallel to mystery cult pharmacological rite: die → be reborn (same day) = death-dose + antidote

Author: Josephus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] οὐδὲν ἔξωθεν ἐσθίουσιν οὐδὲ πίνουσιν ἀλλ' ὅσα οἱ ἐπιμεληταὶ παρέχουσιν
English_Translation: They [Essenes] eat and drink nothing from outside except what the curators provide
Substance_Described: Essene ritual food control (no drug specified)
Ritual_Context: Essene community meals; strict purity rules; initiatory hierarchy
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Control of ingested substances as religious practice; what you eat/drink = spiritual status
Notes: Essene food control parallels mystery cult control of kykeon; possible pharmacological awareness; 3-year initiation

Author: Josephus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] ἐκ μέθης... ἡ ψυχὴ ἐλευθερωθεῖσα... ἐπανέρχεται
English_Translation: As from drunkenness... the soul, having been freed... returns
Substance_Described: Sleep as death-analogue; drunkenness as death-analogue (μέθη)
Ritual_Context: Eleazar's philosophical speech at Masada; death-contemplation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Soul leaves body in sleep as death rehearsal; drunkenness = pharmacological death-simulation; soul returns = resurrection
Notes: Jewish Hellenistic synthesis: Greek mystery-philosophical language about sleep/drug/death used by Jewish commander

Author: Mark
Greek_Text_Excerpt: καὶ ἐδίδουν αὐτῷ ἐσμυρνισμένον οἶνον· ὃς δὲ οὐκ ἔλαβεν
English_Translation: And they offered him myrrh-mixed wine; but he did not take it
Ritual_Context: Crucifixion; possibly merciful preparation before death
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Jesus REFUSES the death-analgesic/narcotic preparation; chooses to die fully conscious
Notes: Myrrh = standard anointing/burial substance; also pharmacologically active; refusing it = refusing pharmacological death-mitigation

Author: Matthew
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ἔδωκαν αὐτῷ πιεῖν οἶνον μετὰ χολῆς μεμιγμένον· καὶ γευσάμενος οὐκ ἤθελεν πίειν
English_Translation: They gave him wine mixed with gall to drink; having tasted he would not drink
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Bitter herb compound; possibly Citrullus colocynthis (colocynth) or Artemisia absinthium
Ritual_Context: Crucifixion preparation; death-inducing/analgesic offered
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Tasting then refusing: Jesus recognized and rejected the pharmacological preparation
Notes: χολή in LXX = death/punishment (Jer 9:15); structural: two death-pharmaka offered and refused

Author: John
Greek_Text_Excerpt: σκεῦος ἔκειτο ὄξους μεστόν· σπόγγον οὖν μεστὸν τοῦ ὄξους ὑσσώπῳ περιθέντες προσήνεγκαν αὐτοῦ τῷ στόματι
English_Translation: A vessel full of sour wine stood there; placing a sponge full of sour wine on hyssop they brought it to his mouth
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Acetum (posca — Roman soldier's drink); possibly spiked with mandrake/opium
Ritual_Context: Crucifixion; liturgically freighted (hyssop = purification in Passover and LXX)
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Jesus ACCEPTS ὄξος → immediately dies; in sequence: refuse death-A → refuse death-B → accept C → die
Notes: If sponge contained mandrake/opium masked by vinegar: pharmacological death sequence; hyssop = LXX purification plant (Ps 51:7; Ex 12:22)

Author: John
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ἦλθεν δὲ καὶ Νικόδημος... φέρων μίγμα σμύρνης καὶ ἀλόης ὡς λίτρας ἑκατόν
English_Translation: Nicodemus came... bringing a mixture of myrrh and aloe, about a hundred litrai
Substance_Described: Myrrh + aloe burial mixture (100 litrai = ~33 kg)
Substance_Latin_Scientific: Commiphora myrrha + Aloe vera / Aquilaria agallocha
Ritual_Context: Jewish burial preparation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Massive quantity of pharmacologically active substances applied at burial; myrrh = analgesic; aloe = potentially Aquilaria (agarwood incense)
Notes: Quantity (100 litrai) far exceeds normal embalming needs; pharmacological density of burial narrative deserves study

Author: NT/LXX
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ἔδωκέν μοι... γνῶσιν τῶν ὄντων... δυνάμεις ῥιζῶν... καὶ φαρμάκων ἐπίσκεψιν
English_Translation: He [God] gave me knowledge of existing things... powers of roots... and examination of pharmaka
Ritual_Context: Solomonic wisdom tradition; pharmacological knowledge as divine gift
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Pharmacological knowledge (ῥίζαι) given by God = sacred; places pharmaka within Jewish-Hellenistic theological framework
Notes: LXX Wisdom combines Greek pharmacological tradition with Jewish theological framing; available to Jesus as scripture

Author: Theophrastus
Greek_Text_Excerpt: ἐλλέβορος λευκός... δραστικώτερον φάρμακον καὶ χαλεπώτερον
English_Translation: White hellebore... a more active drug and more dangerous
Ritual_Context: Cure for madness (Melampus myth); purgative initiation
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Purging = death-like crisis → restored sanity = rebirth pattern; Melampus myth = pharmacological initiation charter
Notes: Melampus tradition: hellebore cures divine madness (of Proetus's daughters) = pharmacological healing of divine punishment

Author: Strabo
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [prose] ἡ Μήδεια... φαρμακεία... τὰ τοῦ Πόντου φάρμακα
English_Translation: Medea... her drug-craft... the pharmaka of Pontus
Ritual_Context: mandrake
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: etc.)
Notes: Colchian/Medea mythological tradition; geographical pharmacological source
column16: Strabo confirms Pontus as geographical origin of Greek pharmakological tradition; Medea = Pontic = Mithridatic lineage
column17: Pontus → Medea myth → Mithridates VI (also Pontic king): geographical continuity of pharmacological tradition

Author: Pliny the Elder
Greek_Text_Excerpt: [Latin] mandragorae radix... per annum in vino macerata... ante sectiones et puncturas
English_Translation: The root of mandrake... soaked in wine for a year... given before cuttings and piercings
Ritual_Context: Surgical anesthesia; Roman medical practice
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Same procedure as Dioscorides and Theophrastus: anesthetic death-sleep for surgery; awakening = resurrection
Notes: By 1st c. CE mandrake wine is standard surgical anesthetic across Greek-Roman medicine

Author: Euripides
Greek_Text_Excerpt: τεκεῖν σέ φημι παῖδας, ὧν ἔχω φάρμακα
English_Translation: I will make you father children; I have the pharmaka for it
Ritual_Context: Bargain with Aegeus; pharmaka as exchange currency
Death_Resurrection_Relevance: Life-giving pole of pharmacological spectrum: fertility drugs opposite to death-drugs; Medea controls both poles
Notes: Medea's power = full life/death pharmaceutical spectrum; she can give life (fertility) or take it (poison)
